name: GPD Physics Benchmark — Statistical Mechanics
description: Benchmark tasks from statistical mechanics: partition functions, phase transitions, critical phenomena, and exactly solved models.

tasks.title: Partition function of the 1D Ising model
tasks.description: Compute the exact partition function of the one-dimensional Ising model using the transfer matrix method.
tasks.problem_statement: For the 1D Ising model with N spins, nearest-neighbor coupling J, and external field h, compute the partition function Z using the transfer matrix method. Take periodic boundary conditions. Show that there is no phase transition at finite temperature.
given: Hamiltonian: H = -J sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i s_{i+1} - h sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i
Transfer matrix: T = [[exp(beta*J + beta*h), exp(-beta*J)], [exp(-beta*J), exp(beta*J - beta*h)]]
tasks.expected_answer: Z = lambda_+^N + lambda_-^N where lambda_{+/-} = exp(beta*J) * [cosh(beta*h) +/- sqrt(sinh^2(beta*h) + exp(-4*beta*J))]
verification_hints: At h=0: Z = (2 cosh(beta*J))^N + (2 sinh(beta*J))^N
No phase transition at finite T: free energy is analytic for all T > 0
In the thermodynamic limit N -> inf: Z -> lambda_+^N
At T=0, h=0: all spins align, ground state energy = -NJ

tasks.title: Mean-field critical exponents for the Ising model
tasks.description: Derive the critical exponents of the Ising model in mean-field (Landau) theory.
tasks.problem_statement: Using Landau's free energy expansion F = a_0 + a_2(T) m^2 + a_4 m^4 - h*m with a_2(T) = a(T - T_c), derive the mean-field critical exponents alpha, beta, gamma, and delta for the Ising model.
given: Landau free energy: F(m, T, h) = a_0 + a(T - T_c) m^2 + b m^4 - h m
assumptions: Analytic free energy expansion (valid near T_c)
conventions: Standard critical exponent definitions: C ~ |t|^{-alpha}, m ~ |t|^beta, chi ~ |t|^{-gamma}, m ~ h^{1/delta} at T = T_c
verification_hints: Check the Rushbrooke inequality: alpha + 2*beta + gamma >= 2 (here: 0 + 1 + 1 = 2, saturated)
Check the Widom relation: gamma = beta*(delta - 1) (here: 1 = (1/2)*2, satisfied)
These are the exact exponents for d >= 4 (upper critical dimension)
For the 3D Ising model, the actual exponents differ: beta approx 0.326, gamma approx 1.237

tasks.title: Specific heat of a 2D Debye solid
tasks.description: Derive the temperature dependence of the specific heat of a 2D solid in the Debye model at low and high temperatures.
tasks.problem_statement: For a two-dimensional Debye solid with N atoms and Debye temperature Theta_D, determine the specific heat C(T) in the limits T >> Theta_D and T << Theta_D.
given: Debye density of states in 2D: g(omega) = 2N omega / omega_D^2 for omega < omega_D
Debye frequency: omega_D defined by integral of g(omega) = 2N (two polarizations)
assumptions: Debye approximation: linear dispersion up to cutoff omega_D
tasks.expected_answer: High T (T >> Theta_D): C = 2Nk_B (2D equipartition). Low T (T << Theta_D): C ~ T^2 (Debye T^d law in d=2).
verification_hints: In d dimensions, the Debye low-T specific heat goes as C ~ T^d
In 3D the famous result is C ~ T^3, so in 2D it is C ~ T^2
High-T limit must recover classical equipartition: C = d*N*k_B per degree of freedom
Check: the crossover temperature scale is T ~ Theta_D

tasks.title: Entropy of mixing for ideal gases
tasks.description: Calculate the entropy of mixing when two ideal gases at the same T and P are combined, and resolve the Gibbs paradox.
tasks.problem_statement: Two containers hold ideal gases A and B, each with N molecules at temperature T and pressure P. A partition is removed and the gases mix. Calculate the entropy change. Then consider the case where both gases are identical (A = B) and explain the resolution of the Gibbs paradox.
given: Gas A: N molecules in volume V
Gas B: N molecules in volume V
Both at the same T and P
assumptions: Same temperature and pressure before mixing
conventions: S = k_B ln Omega for microcanonical entropy
tasks.expected_answer: For distinguishable gases: Delta S = 2 N k_B ln 2. For identical gases: Delta S = 0.
verification_hints: The entropy of mixing for distinguishable gases must be positive (second law)
For identical gases, removing the partition is not a thermodynamic process
The Gibbs paradox is resolved by proper counting (dividing by N!) in quantum statistics
For a mixture of n_A and n_B with total N = n_A + n_B: Delta S = -k_B [x_A ln x_A + x_B ln x_B] * N

tasks.title: Stefan-Boltzmann law from Planck distribution
tasks.description: Derive the Stefan-Boltzmann law for total radiated power from the Planck blackbody spectrum.
tasks.problem_statement: Starting from the Planck spectral energy density u(nu, T), integrate over all frequencies to obtain the total energy density u(T) of blackbody radiation, thereby deriving the Stefan-Boltzmann law and expressing the Stefan-Boltzmann constant sigma in terms of fundamental constants.
given: Planck spectral energy density: u(nu, T) = (8 pi h nu^3)/(c^3) * 1/(exp(h nu/(k_B T)) - 1)
conventions: Power per unit area: P/A = sigma T^4 where sigma is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant
tasks.expected_answer: u(T) = (8 pi^5 k_B^4)/(15 c^3 h^3) * T^4. Stefan-Boltzmann constant: sigma = (2 pi^5 k_B^4)/(15 c^2 h^3).
verification_hints: Check dimensions: [u] = energy/volume = J/m^3
The T^4 dependence follows from the substitution x = h nu/(k_B T)
Wien's displacement law (peak frequency ~ T) is a separate consequence of the Planck spectrum
